title: Passionflower
subtitle: The nerve-calming herb vine of the Eclectic tradition
excerpt: Passiflora incarnata, a climbing vine native to the south-eastern United States, documented in Eclectic American and native practice for mild anxiety and restless sleep. Among the most studied nerve-calming herb herbs in clinical use.
sourceNotes: King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Passiflora incarnata entry: the nerve-calming herb and muscle-relaxing actions, the traditional Eclectic use for insomnia and nervous restlessness. the herbal reference on Passiflorae herba, the evidence review for mild anxiety and sleep support. the World Health Organisation Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 3, Herba Passiflorae.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that calms or tones the nervous system. Nervines range from gentle (chamomile, lemon balm) to more substantial (valerian, passionflower). The term is traditional rather than pharmacological.
An action associated with reducing mild anxiety. In herbal medicine, the term describes a traditional use rather than a medical claim.

content.text: Passionflower (Passiflora incarnata) is a climbing vine native to the south-eastern United States. The flowers are striking: white petals beneath a corona of purple-and-white filaments (this gave the plant its name, reminding early observers of the Passion). The fruits (maypops) are edible. For herbal medicine, the aerial parts (leaf, stem, flower) are used.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Sedatives. Passionflower potentiates benzodiazepines and other CNS drugs. Do not combine with prescription sedatives, sleeping tablets, or other sedating medication without consulting your prescriber.

Pregnancy. Passionflower is avoided in pregnancy at therapeutic doses. Consult a herbalist or your midwife before using any passionflower preparation in pregnancy.

Driving. Because passionflower can cause drowsiness in sensitive individuals, avoid driving or operating machinery after taking therapeutic doses, particularly when first using the herb.

Paediatric use. Not for children under twelve.

content.text: Latin binomial: Passiflora incarnata. Family: Passifloraceae. Parts used: aerial parts (leaf, stem, flower), dried. The vine climbs by tendrils, reaching 3-9 metres. The leaves are deeply three-lobed. The flower lasts only a day. Harvested in summer when flowering; the whole above-ground parts are dried for use. P. incarnata is the medicinal species; ornamental garden passionflowers (P. caerulea and others) are different plants and should not be substituted.
The flavonoids (chrysin, orientin, vitexin) are the primary studied active compounds, alongside harman alkaloids and iridoid compounds. The

actions are the ones western herbalists draw on. The muscle-relaxing action (relaxing smooth muscle) is noted in Eclectic tradition for restless nervous conditions accompanied by muscular tension. Limited study evidence supports passionflower for anxiety. Larger trials are still needed.

Native American peoples of the south-eastern US used passionflower root for boils and ear problems. The Eclectic physicians of 19th-century America established the nerve-calming tradition with this herb. Their dispensatory describes it for insomnia, nervous restlessness, and neuralgia. They noted its value for sleeplessness from mental over-activity.

Western herbal medicine adopted the Eclectic use. Passionflower became a standard European nerve-calming herb through the 20th century. Traditional use covers relief of mild mental stress and sleep aid. This is one of the stronger evidence-supported nerve-calming herb herbs available to the home herbalist.

The most common preparations are infusion (2-4 g dried herb in 150 ml hot water, steeped covered 8-10 minutes), herbal spirit extraction (1:5 in 40% spirit, 2-4 ml), and dry extract capsules. the herbal reference's reference dose for the infusion is 2-4 g dried herb daily. Passionflower blends well with other nervines: valerian for sleep emphasis, lemon balm for digestive nervous conditions, hops where mild sedation is the aim.

Sedatives: do not combine with prescription CNS depressants. Pregnancy: avoid at therapeutic doses. Driving: first use should be taken in the evening. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve. Clinical anxiety, depression, or panic disorder should be assessed by a doctor or mental health professional; passionflower is appropriate for mild stress and occasional sleeplessness, not for clinical conditions.
